docs.title: RFC 9259 - Operations and Management of SRv6
docs.text: Field Value Title Operations, Administration, and Maintenance (OAM) in Segment Routing over IPv6 (SRv6) Authors Z. Ali, C. Filsfils, S. Matsushima, D. Voyer, M. Chen Date June 2022 Status Proposed Standard Link datatracker.ietf.org/doc/rfc9259

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: RFC 9259 defines the OAM mechanisms for SRv6 networks. It specifies how existing OAM tools (ping, traceroute) work in an SRv6 environment and defines new SRv6-specific OAM behaviors.

docs.title: SRv6 OAM Behaviors
docs.text: Behavior Description End.OP OAM endpoint - punt packet to control plane for OAM processing End.OTP OAM endpoint with timestamp - adds timestamp before punting

docs.title: Ping in SRv6
docs.text: SRv6 ping uses ICMPv6 Echo Request/Reply with the SRH to verify reachability along an SRv6 path. The ping follows the segment list, allowing operators to verify end-to-end SRv6 connectivity.

docs.title: Traceroute in SRv6
docs.text: SRv6 traceroute works by incrementing the IPv6 Hop Limit to discover each hop along the SRv6 path. Each intermediate node returns an ICMPv6 Time Exceeded message, revealing the path taken.

docs.title: Key Differences from Traditional OAM
docs.text:
• OAM packets carry the SRH to follow the same path as data traffic
  • Intermediate nodes may or may not be SRv6-aware
  • SRv6 OAM can validate both the underlay path and the overlay service

docs.title: Key Sections
docs.text: Section Topic Description 3 OAM Flag in SRH Defines the O-flag (bit 0) in the SRH Flags field; when set, it indicates the packet is an OAM packet that should be processed by the OAM mechanism at each SRv6-capable node 4 SRv6 OAM Endpoint Behaviors Specifies End.OP (OAM punt) and End.OTP (OAM with Timestamp) endpoint behaviors for OAM processing 5 SRv6 Ping ICMPv6 Echo Request/Reply procedures for SRv6; the source constructs the ping packet with an SRH containing the target segment list 5.1 Ping to a SID Ping a specific SRv6 SID by placing it as the final segment; verifies SID reachability 5.2 Ping to a Policy Ping using the full segment list of an SR Policy; verifies end-to-end path connectivity 6 SRv6 Traceroute Hop-by-hop discovery using incrementing IPv6 Hop Limit with SRH; each hop returns ICMPv6 Time Exceeded 6.1 Traceroute to a SID Discovers the path to a specific SRv6 SID 6.2 Traceroute to a Policy Discovers all hops along an SR Policy path 7 Security Considerations Rate limiting, trust boundaries, and filtering of OAM packets

docs.title: OAM Flag Detail
docs.text:
The O-flag (Bit 0 in the SRH Flags field) marks a packet as an OAM packet:

SRH Flags:
 0 1 2 3 4 5 6 7
+-+-+-+-+-+-+-+-+
|O|    Unused   |
+-+-+-+-+-+-+-+-+

When the O-flag is set, an SR endpoint node may choose to process the packet as OAM (e.g., punt to control plane) rather than forwarding it in the data plane.

Common Pitfalls

Rate limiting ICMPv6 is critical. SRv6 OAM relies on ICMPv6 Echo and Time Exceeded messages. Without proper rate limiting, OAM traffic can be used for denial-of-service attacks or can overwhelm the control plane of transit and endpoint nodes. Implementations must apply ICMPv6 rate limiting consistent with RFC 4443.

Transit nodes may not respond to traceroute. In SRv6, transit nodes (non-segment-endpoint routers between SRv6 nodes) forward packets based only on the IPv6 DA. They do not process the SRH and may not generate ICMPv6 Time Exceeded messages that include the SRH. This means traceroute output may show transit hops but without SRv6-specific context. Some transit nodes may even suppress ICMPv6 responses entirely.

O-flag processing is optional. Not all SRv6 implementations process the O-flag. An endpoint that does not support OAM behaviors (End.OP / End.OTP) will process the packet as a regular data packet despite the flag being set. Operators must verify OAM support on all relevant nodes.

Ping to a SID verifies the control plane, not necessarily the data plane. A successful SRv6 ping confirms that the SID is reachable and the node responds, but it does not guarantee that data traffic following the same segment list will be forwarded correctly (e.g., VRF binding, L2 cross-connect).

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• Path verification - Confirm traffic follows the intended SRv6 policy
  • Fault isolation - Identify which segment in the path is failing
  • Performance monitoring - Measure latency and loss per segment
  • SLA validation - Verify service-level agreements on SRv6 paths

docs.title: RFC 9350 - IGP Flexible Algorithm
docs.text: Field Value Title IGP Flexible Algorithm Authors P. Psenak, S. Hegde, C. Filsfils, K. Talaulikar, A. Gulko Date February 2023 Status Proposed Standard Link datatracker.ietf.org/doc/rfc9350

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
RFC 9350 defines the IGP Flexible Algorithm (Flex-Algo) framework, which allows IGPs (IS-IS and OSPF) to compute constraint-based paths without requiring an external controller or RSVP-TE signaling. Each Flexible Algorithm is identified by a numeric value in the range 128-255 and defines a set of computation parameters: a metric type, a calculation type, and optional constraints such as link affinities.

Flex-Algo enables operators to create multiple logical topologies over a single physical network. For example, Algorithm 128 might compute shortest paths based on latency while Algorithm 129 computes paths that avoid certain link colors. Nodes that participate in a given algorithm advertise their participation and compute the corresponding shortest path tree independently, using the distributed IGP.

The framework is data-plane agnostic and works with both SR-MPLS (via Prefix SIDs with an algorithm field) and SRv6 (via per-algorithm SRv6 Locators). This makes Flex-Algo one of the most widely deployed mechanisms for network slicing and traffic engineering in SR networks.

docs.title: Key Sections
docs.text: Section Topic Description 3 Flex-Algorithm Definition The advertisement containing metric type, calc-type, and constraints 4 Flex-Algorithm Participation How a node signals it supports a given algorithm 5 Flex-Algorithm Definition Election How the winning definition is selected (highest router ID) 6 Metric Types IGP metric, TE metric, minimum unidirectional link delay 7 Calculation Types SPF (0) and Strict SPF (1) 8 Affinity Constraints Include-any, include-all, exclude-any for link affinities

docs.title: Algorithm IDs
docs.text: Range Assignment 0 Standard SPF (default) 1 Strict SPF 2-127 Reserved by IANA 128-255 Flexible Algorithms (operator-defined)

docs.title: Flex-Algo Definition (FAD) Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Description Example Algorithm ID in range 128-255 128 Metric Type Which metric to use for SPF 0 = IGP, 1 = Min Unidirectional Delay, 2 = TE Metric Calculation Type Path computation method 0 = SPF Priority Used in definition election 237 Include-Any Affinity Link must have at least one of these colors red, blue Include-All Affinity Link must have all of these colors gold Exclude-Any Affinity Link must not have any of these colors maintenance

docs.title: Flex-Algo with SR-MPLS vs SRv6
docs.text:
Aspect SR-MPLS SRv6 SID binding Prefix SID with Algorithm field set to 128-255 SRv6 Locator advertised under the algorithm IGP advertisement IS-IS: Prefix SID sub-TLV / OSPF: SID/Label sub-TLV IS-IS: SRv6 Locator TLV / OSPFv3: SRv6 Locator LSA Path computation SPF on topology filtered by FAD constraints Same Result Per-algorithm label forwarding table Per-algorithm SRv6 SID table

Common Pitfalls

  • Not all nodes need to participate in every algorithm, but a node that does not participate will not be in the SPF tree for that algorithm, making it unreachable via that Flex-Algo.
  • The Flex-Algorithm Definition (FAD) must be advertised by at least one node. The definition with the highest advertising router ID wins the election -- not the lowest.
  • Misconfiguring affinity constraints can partition the Flex-Algo topology, leaving some nodes unreachable. Always verify end-to-end connectivity in the constrained topology.
  • Forgetting to assign the correct admin-group (affinity) colors to links before deploying a Flex-Algo that references them.
  • Using delay-based metric (Type 1) without deploying link-delay measurement. Without actual delay values, the metric defaults to maximum and paths become unpredictable.

docs.title: RFC 9352 - IS-IS Extensions for SRv6
docs.text: Field Value Title IS-IS Extensions to Support Segment Routing over the IPv6 Data Plane Authors P. Psenak, C. Filsfils, A. Bashandy, B. Decraene, Z. Hu Date February 2023 Status Proposed Standard Link datatracker.ietf.org/doc/rfc9352

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: RFC 9352 defines extensions to the IS-IS routing protocol to support SRv6. These extensions allow IS-IS to advertise SRv6 SIDs, locators, and endpoint behaviors, enabling automated SRv6 deployment within an IGP domain.

docs.title: SRv6 Locator Advertisement
docs.text:
IS-IS advertises SRv6 locators as reachable IPv6 prefixes with additional SRv6-specific TLVs. This allows all routers in the IS-IS domain to learn:

  • The SRv6 locator prefix for each node
  • The SRv6 SIDs and their associated behaviors
  • The algorithm used for path computation

docs.title: New TLVs and Sub-TLVs
docs.text: TLV/Sub-TLV Description SRv6 Locator TLV (27) Advertises an SRv6 locator with its prefix and flags SRv6 End SID Sub-TLV Associates an End SID with a locator SRv6 End.X SID Sub-TLV Advertises adjacency-specific SIDs SRv6 SID Structure Sub-TLV Describes the locator/function/argument split SRv6 Capabilities Sub-TLV Advertises SRv6 capabilities of a node

docs.title: SRv6 SID Structure
docs.text:
The SID Structure sub-TLV communicates how a 128-bit SID is divided:

|<-- Locator Block -->|<-- Locator Node -->|<-- Function -->|<-- Argument -->|
|<------- Locator Length (bits) -------->|

This allows other nodes to understand the structure of SIDs without pre-configuration.

docs.title: Key Sections
docs.text: Section Topic Description 2 SRv6 Locator TLV (Type 27) Top-level IS-IS TLV that advertises an SRv6 locator prefix, its metric, algorithm, and flags 2.1 SRv6 End SID Sub-TLV Sub-TLV of the Locator TLV; associates an End SID with a locator and specifies endpoint behavior 2.2 SRv6 End.X SID Sub-TLV Advertises adjacency-specific SIDs (layer-3 cross-connect); carried in the Extended IS Reachability TLV 2.3 SRv6 LAN End.X SID Sub-TLV End.X SID for multi-access (LAN) segments with a neighbor system-ID identifier 3 SRv6 SID Structure Sub-TLV Describes the bit-level decomposition of the SID: Locator Block Length, Locator Node Length, Function Length, Argument Length 4 SRv6 Capabilities Sub-TLV Advertises SRv6 capability flags within the IS-IS Router Capability TLV (type 242) 5 Flex-Algorithm Integration SRv6 locators can be associated with Flex-Algorithm IDs, enabling topology/constraint-aware SID advertisement 6 Security Considerations IS-IS authentication protects SRv6 TLV integrity; no additional mechanisms required beyond standard IS-IS security

docs.title: SRv6 Locator TLV Detail
docs.text:
The SRv6 Locator TLV (Type 27) structure:

Field Length Description Metric 4 bytes Route metric for the locator prefix Flags 1 byte D-flag (leaking from L2 to L1) Algorithm 1 byte IGP Algorithm (0 = SPF, 128-255 = Flex-Algorithm) Locator Prefix Length 1 byte Prefix length in bits Locator Prefix Variable The SRv6 locator IPv6 prefix Sub-TLVs Variable End SID sub-TLVs, SID Structure sub-TLVs

docs.title: SRv6 SID Structure Sub-TLV Detail
docs.text:
Field Length Description Locator Block Length (LBL) 1 byte Number of bits for the Locator Block (common across the domain) Locator Node Length (LNL) 1 byte Number of bits for the Locator Node (per-node identifier) Function Length (FL) 1 byte Number of bits for the Function (behavior-specific) Argument Length (AL) 1 byte Number of bits for the Argument (optional, behavior-specific)

SID Structure Example

For a SID fc00:0:1::1 with block fc00::/24, node 0:0:1::/40, function ::1/16:

  • Locator Block Length = 24
  • Locator Node Length = 16
  • Function Length = 16
  • Argument Length = 0

Common Pitfalls

Locator Block Length vs Locator Node Length. The Locator Block is the common prefix shared across the SRv6 domain (e.g., fc00::/24), while the Locator Node is the per-node portion within the block. Together they form the full Locator. Confusing these two fields leads to incorrect SID resolution. The total Locator Length = Locator Block Length + Locator Node Length.

SID Structure mismatch causes silent failures. If the SID Structure sub-TLV values do not match the actual SID allocation on the advertising node, remote nodes will parse the SID incorrectly. For example, if a node advertises Function Length = 16 but actually uses 20 bits for the function, other nodes will compute wrong SIDs for VPN services.

Algorithm field must match Flex-Algorithm. When advertising a locator under a Flex-Algorithm (algorithm value 128-255), the Algorithm field in the Locator TLV must match the Flex-Algorithm Definition advertised elsewhere in IS-IS (RFC 9350). A mismatch means the locator will not be used for that algorithm's path computation.

End.X SIDs are per-adjacency, not per-locator. End.X SIDs are advertised in the Extended IS Reachability TLV (TLV 22 or TLV 222) as sub-TLVs of the adjacency, not as sub-TLVs of the Locator TLV. This is different from End SIDs which are sub-TLVs of the Locator TLV.

docs.title: Interaction with Flex-Algorithm
docs.text: SRv6 locators can be associated with IS-IS Flex-Algorithm, enabling constraint-based path computation. Different locators can be advertised under different algorithms (e.g., shortest path, low latency, disjoint paths).

docs.title: Configuration Example (Cisco IOS-XR)
docs.text:
router isis CORE
 address-family ipv6 unicast
  segment-routing srv6
   locator MAIN
  !
 !
!

docs.title: Configuration Example (Juniper)
docs.text: set protocols isis source-packet-routing srv6 locator myloc end-sid fc00:0:2::1

docs.title: RFC 9433 - Segment Routing over IPv6 for the Mobile User Plane
docs.text: Field Value Title Segment Routing over IPv6 for the Mobile User Plane Authors S. Matsushima, C. Filsfils, M. Kohno, P. Camarillo, D. Voyer, C. Perkins Date July 2023 Status Proposed Standard Link datatracker.ietf.org/doc/rfc9433

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
RFC 9433 defines how SRv6 can be used as the mobile user plane protocol in 3GPP networks, replacing GTP-U tunneling between mobile network functions. By encoding mobile session state directly into SRv6 SIDs, the architecture eliminates the need for GTP-U encapsulation between the UPF (User Plane Function) nodes, reducing per-packet overhead and simplifying the data plane.

The document specifies several new SRv6 endpoint behaviors purpose-built for mobile user plane interworking. These behaviors handle the translation between GTP-U and SRv6 at network boundaries and the pure SRv6 forwarding within the mobile transport. The SID structure carries mobile-specific arguments such as QFI (QoS Flow Identifier) and TEID (Tunnel Endpoint Identifier) information, enabling stateless translation at domain boundaries.

This approach allows operators to converge their mobile backhaul and transport networks onto a single SRv6 infrastructure, reducing operational complexity while enabling advanced capabilities like network slicing using SRv6 and Flex-Algo.

docs.title: Key Sections
docs.text: Section Topic Description 3 Reference Architecture 5G user plane architecture with SRv6 replacing GTP-U 4 SRv6 SID for Mobile User Plane SID structure carrying mobile session context 5 SRv6 Endpoint Behaviors New behaviors for GTP-U interworking 6 Uplink and Downlink Scenarios Packet flow for both directions 7 IPv4 User Plane Support Handling IPv4 UE traffic over SRv6

docs.title: MUP Endpoint Behaviors
docs.text: Behavior Direction Function End.M.GTP6.D Downlink Decapsulates SRv6 and encapsulates into GTP-U toward the gNB (IPv6 payload) End.M.GTP6.Di Downlink Same as above but for IPv6 traffic with inner header insertion End.M.GTP6.E Uplink Decapsulates GTP-U from gNB and encapsulates into SRv6 End.M.GTP4.E Uplink Decapsulates GTP-U (IPv4 outer) and encapsulates into SRv6 H.M.GTP4.D Downlink Headend behavior: encodes GTP-U parameters into SRv6 SID args for IPv4 N3

docs.title: MUP SID Structure
docs.text:
The MUP SID encodes mobile session information directly in the SRv6 SID:

|<-------- Locator -------->|<-- Function -->|<--- Argument --->|
|   Block     |    Node     |    Behavior    |  TEID + QFI etc  |
Field Purpose Locator Identifies the UPF or mobile anchor node Function Identifies the MUP behavior (e.g., End.M.GTP6.D) Argument Carries TEID, QFI, and other GTP-U session parameters

docs.title: GTP-U to SRv6 Interworking
docs.text:
Scenario Source Translation Point Destination Uplink (N3) gNB sends GTP-U Ingress UPF: GTP-U to SRv6 Anchor UPF (SRv6 endpoint) Downlink (N3) Anchor UPF sends SRv6 Egress UPF: SRv6 to GTP-U gNB receives GTP-U N9 (inter-UPF) UPF sends SRv6 No translation needed UPF receives SRv6

Common Pitfalls

  • The MUP behaviors require the argument portion of the SID to be large enough to encode the TEID (32 bits) and QFI (6 bits). An undersized Locator:Function leaves insufficient room for arguments.
  • The interworking functions at domain boundaries must maintain awareness of the GTP-U tunnel state for proper TEID mapping, even though the SRv6 core is stateless.
  • IPv4 user plane traffic requires special handling (End.M.GTP4.E / H.M.GTP4.D) because of the different N3 encapsulation header format.
  • Deploying MUP without Flex-Algo for network slicing loses one of the key benefits -- the ability to map 5G network slices to distinct SRv6 topologies.
  • Not all SRv6 implementations support MUP behaviors. Verify platform support before planning a GTP-U replacement deployment.

docs.title: RFC 9487 - Export of Segment Routing over IPv6 Information in IP Flow Information Export (IPFIX)
docs.text: Field Value Title Export of Segment Routing over IPv6 Information in IP Flow Information Export (IPFIX) Authors T. Graf, B. Claise, P. Francois Date November 2023 Status Informational Link datatracker.ietf.org/doc/rfc9487

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
RFC 9487 defines new IPFIX (IP Flow Information Export) Information Elements for exporting SRv6-related data from network nodes to collectors. As SRv6 deployments grow, operators need visibility into how SRv6 traffic is forwarded -- which SIDs are being used, what segment lists packets carry, and how traffic maps to SR Policies. This RFC provides the telemetry building blocks to answer those questions.

The document specifies 11 new IPFIX Information Elements that capture the essential SRv6 forwarding state observed at a metering point. These elements cover the active SID in the IPv6 destination address, the full segment list from the SRH, the Segments Left value, Section Tag, and flags. Together, they enable a collector to reconstruct the SRv6 forwarding path a packet is following.

These IPFIX elements are essential for SRv6 network operations, enabling use cases such as traffic engineering validation (confirming packets follow the intended SR Policy), SLA monitoring (measuring per-path latency and loss), capacity planning (understanding per-SID traffic volumes), and troubleshooting (identifying packets with unexpected segment lists).

docs.title: Key Sections
docs.text: Section Topic Description 3 Use Cases Traffic engineering verification, SLA monitoring, troubleshooting 4 New IPFIX Information Elements Definition of 11 SRv6-specific IEs 5 Encoding How SRv6 SIDs and segment lists are encoded in IPFIX records 6 Export Scenarios Where in the network SRv6 IPFIX export is most useful

docs.title: New IPFIX Information Elements
docs.text: IE Name IE ID Type Description srhFlags 502 unsigned8 Flags field from the SRH srhTag 503 unsigned16 Tag field from the SRH srhSegmentIPv6 504 ipv6Address An individual SRv6 SID from the segment list srhActiveSegmentIPv6 505 ipv6Address The currently active SID (from IPv6 DA) srhSegmentIPv6BasicList 506 basicList Ordered list of all SRv6 SIDs in the SRH srhSegmentsIPv6Left 507 unsigned8 Segments Left value from the SRH srhIPv6Section 508 unsigned8 Section of the SRH being reported srhIPv6ActiveSegmentType 509 unsigned8 Type of the active segment (e.g., transit, endpoint) srhSegmentIPv6ListSection 510 basicList Section of the segment list for large SRHs srhSegmentsIPv6SectionLeft 511 unsigned8 Segments Left relative to the reported section srhIPv6SRHLength 512 unsigned16 Total length of the SRH in bytes

docs.title: Export Use Cases
docs.text: Use Case Key IEs Used Benefit TE Verification srhSegmentIPv6BasicList, srhActiveSegmentIPv6 Confirm packets follow the programmed SR Policy path SLA Monitoring srhActiveSegmentIPv6 + timestamps Measure per-path latency and jitter Capacity Planning srhSegmentIPv6 + byte/packet counters Understand traffic volumes per SID and segment list Troubleshooting srhFlags, srhSegmentsIPv6Left, full segment list Identify unexpected forwarding behavior or misrouted packets Accounting srhActiveSegmentIPv6 + flow counters Per-service or per-tenant traffic accounting

docs.title: IPFIX Export Points in an SRv6 Network
docs.text:
Export Point What is Visible Typical Use Headend (ingress) Full SRH with complete segment list Verify SR Policy is applied correctly Transit node IPv6 DA (active SID); SRH if configured to inspect Monitor per-SID traffic load Endpoint (egress) Final SID processing, decapsulated inner packet End-to-end path validation

Common Pitfalls

  • Not all hardware supports deep SRH inspection for IPFIX export. Transit routers often only see the IPv6 destination address (active SID) and cannot export the full segment list without SRH parsing support.
  • The srhSegmentIPv6BasicList IE uses the IPFIX basicList structured data type (RFC 6313). Collectors must support structured data to parse this field correctly.
  • Large segment lists may exceed IPFIX template or record size limits. The srhSegmentIPv6ListSection and srhIPv6Section IEs provide a mechanism for sectioned export, but collector support varies.
  • SRv6 compressed SIDs (uSID, RFC 9800) appear as standard 128-bit IPv6 addresses in IPFIX. The collector needs additional context (the GIB prefix and C-SID length) to decompose containers into individual micro-SIDs.
  • IPFIX sampling configuration significantly affects telemetry accuracy for SRv6. Low sampling rates may miss short-lived or low-volume SRv6 flows entirely.

docs.title: RFC 9513 - OSPFv3 Extensions for SRv6
docs.text: Field Value Title OSPFv3 Extensions for Segment Routing over IPv6 (SRv6) Authors Z. Li, Z. Hu, K. Talaulikar, P. Psenak Date December 2023 Status Proposed Standard Link datatracker.ietf.org/doc/rfc9513

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
RFC 9513 defines the OSPFv3 extensions required to support Segment Routing over IPv6 (SRv6). While IS-IS SRv6 extensions were defined in RFC 9352, many networks rely on OSPFv3 as their IPv6 link-state routing protocol, and this document provides the equivalent functionality for OSPF-based deployments.

The specification introduces new TLVs and sub-TLVs within OSPFv3 Extended LSAs to advertise SRv6 capabilities, SRv6 Locators, SRv6 End SIDs, and SRv6 Adjacency SIDs. It builds on the OSPFv3 Extended LSA framework defined in RFC 8362, which provides a flexible TLV-based encoding for OSPFv3 advertisements.

The extensions enable OSPFv3 to distribute all the information needed for SRv6 forwarding: node capabilities, locator prefixes, endpoint behaviors (SID functions), and adjacency SIDs for traffic engineering. Combined with Flex-Algo support (RFC 9350), these extensions allow OSPFv3 to fully participate in SRv6 network deployments.

docs.title: Key Sections
docs.text: Section Topic Description 3 SRv6 Capabilities TLV Advertising node-level SRv6 support and flags 4 SRv6 Locator TLV Advertising SRv6 locator prefixes in the E-Router-LSA 5 SRv6 End SID sub-TLV Associating endpoint behaviors with locators 6 SRv6 Adjacency SID sub-TLV Advertising per-link SRv6 adjacency SIDs 7 SRv6 SID Structure sub-TLV Describing the LOC:FUNC:ARG bit-length structure of a SID

docs.title: New TLVs and Sub-TLVs
docs.text: TLV/Sub-TLV Type Carried In Purpose SRv6 Capabilities TLV Router Information LSA Advertise SRv6 support and capability flags SRv6 Locator TLV E-Router-LSA Advertise an SRv6 Locator prefix with algorithm and metric SRv6 End SID Sub-TLV SRv6 Locator TLV Bind an SRv6 endpoint behavior (e.g., End, End.X) to a SID SRv6 Adjacency SID Sub-TLV E-Router-LSA link entries Advertise per-adjacency SRv6 SIDs (End.X equivalent) SRv6 SID Structure Sub-TLV SRv6 End SID / Adj SID Describe the Locator Block, Locator Node, Function, and Argument bit lengths

docs.title: SRv6 Locator Advertisement
docs.text:
The SRv6 Locator TLV carries:

Field Description Route Type Intra-area or inter-area locator Algorithm 0 for SPF, 1 for Strict SPF, 128-255 for Flex-Algo Locator Prefix The IPv6 prefix representing the SRv6 Locator Metric Cost associated with reaching this locator Flags N-flag (node SID), A-flag (no-algorithm)

docs.title: SRv6 SID Structure Sub-TLV
docs.text: Field Bits Description Locator Block Length (LBL) Variable Length of the SRv6 SID Locator Block in bits Locator Node Length (LNL) Variable Length of the Locator Node identifier in bits Function Length (FL) Variable Length of the Function field in bits Argument Length (AL) Variable Length of the Argument field in bits

docs.title: OSPFv3 SRv6 vs IS-IS SRv6
docs.text:
Aspect OSPFv3 (RFC 9513) IS-IS (RFC 9352) LSA/LSP type E-Router-LSA (RFC 8362) Standard IS-IS LSP Capabilities SRv6 Capabilities TLV in RI LSA SRv6 Capabilities sub-TLV in Router Capability TLV Locator SRv6 Locator TLV in E-Router-LSA SRv6 Locator TLV (top-level) End SID Sub-TLV of SRv6 Locator TLV SRv6 End SID sub-TLV of Locator TLV Adjacency SID Sub-TLV of link in E-Router-LSA SRv6 End.X SID sub-TLV in extended IS reachability Flex-Algo Algorithm field in Locator TLV Algorithm field in Locator TLV

Common Pitfalls

  • OSPFv3 SRv6 extensions require Extended LSAs (RFC 8362). Legacy OSPFv3 LSA types cannot carry the new TLVs. Ensure all routers support and are configured for Extended LSAs.
  • The SRv6 SID Structure sub-TLV is optional but strongly recommended. Without it, remote nodes must infer the LOC:FUNC:ARG boundaries, which may lead to interoperability issues.
  • OSPFv3 area boundaries affect SRv6 locator reachability. Inter-area locator advertisements require proper ABR configuration.
  • Do not confuse OSPFv3 SR extensions (RFC 8666, for SR-MPLS) with OSPFv3 SRv6 extensions (RFC 9513). They serve different data planes.
  • When deploying both IS-IS and OSPFv3 for SRv6, ensure consistent locator and SID advertisements to avoid forwarding inconsistencies.

docs.title: RFC 9514 - Border Gateway Protocol - Link State (BGP-LS) Extensions for SRv6
docs.text: Field Value Title Border Gateway Protocol - Link State (BGP-LS) Extensions for Segment Routing over IPv6 (SRv6) Authors G. Dawra, C. Filsfils, K. Talaulikar, M. Chen, D. Bernier, B. Decraene Date December 2023 Status Proposed Standard Link datatracker.ietf.org/doc/rfc9514

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
RFC 9514 defines extensions to the Border Gateway Protocol - Link State (BGP-LS) for carrying SRv6 information. BGP-LS (RFC 7752, updated by RFC 9552) is used to export link-state topology and traffic engineering information from IGP domains to external consumers such as SDN controllers and Path Computation Elements (PCEs). This RFC extends BGP-LS to include SRv6-specific attributes.

The extensions enable a centralized controller to learn the complete SRv6 topology of the network, including SRv6 capabilities, locators, End SIDs, adjacency SIDs (End.X), and the SID structure. With this information, a controller can compute SRv6 paths and program SR Policies without needing to participate in the IGP directly.

This is a critical building block for SDN-based SRv6 deployments where a PCE or SDN controller computes paths centrally and distributes them via PCEP or BGP SR Policy. The BGP-LS SRv6 extensions ensure the controller has a complete and accurate view of the SRv6 data plane.

docs.title: Key Sections
docs.text: Section Topic Description 3 SRv6 Capabilities Node-level SRv6 support advertisement via BGP-LS 4 SRv6 Locator Prefix NLRI attribute carrying locator and algorithm 5 SRv6 End SID BGP-LS attribute TLV for SRv6 endpoint behaviors 6 SRv6 Adjacency SID (End.X) BGP-LS link attribute for per-adjacency SRv6 SIDs 7 SRv6 SID Structure TLV describing the LOC:FUNC:ARG bit layout

docs.title: BGP-LS SRv6 Attribute TLVs
docs.text: TLV Code Point BGP-LS Attribute Maps From (IGP) SRv6 Capabilities 1106 Node Attribute IS-IS / OSPFv3 SRv6 Capabilities SRv6 Locator 1162 Prefix Attribute IS-IS SRv6 Locator TLV / OSPFv3 SRv6 Locator TLV SRv6 End SID 1163 Prefix Attribute IS-IS SRv6 End SID sub-TLV / OSPFv3 SRv6 End SID sub-TLV SRv6 SID Structure 1164 Prefix/Link Attribute IS-IS / OSPFv3 SRv6 SID Structure sub-TLV SRv6 End.X SID 1106 Link Attribute IS-IS SRv6 End.X SID sub-TLV / OSPFv3 SRv6 Adj SID sub-TLV

docs.title: BGP-LS to IGP Mapping
docs.text: BGP-LS Component IS-IS Source OSPFv3 Source Node NLRI + SRv6 Caps Router Capability TLV Router Information LSA Prefix NLRI + SRv6 Locator SRv6 Locator TLV SRv6 Locator TLV in E-Router-LSA Prefix NLRI + SRv6 End SID SRv6 End SID sub-TLV SRv6 End SID sub-TLV Link NLRI + SRv6 End.X SID SRv6 End.X SID sub-TLV SRv6 Adjacency SID sub-TLV

docs.title: Use with SDN Controllers and PCE
docs.text:
+------------------+
|  SDN Controller  |     BGP-LS provides the SRv6 topology database
|  / PCE           |     to the controller, which then computes
+--------+---------+     paths and programs SR Policies via PCEP
         |                or BGP SR Policy.
    BGP-LS Session
         |
+--------+---------+
|   BGP-LS Speaker |     Collects IGP SRv6 data from IS-IS/OSPFv3
|   (Route Reflector)    and exports via BGP-LS to the controller.
+--------+---------+
         |
    IGP Domain (IS-IS or OSPFv3 with SRv6 extensions)
Component Role BGP-LS Speaker Collects SRv6 information from the IGP and exports it northbound SDN Controller / PCE Consumes the BGP-LS SRv6 topology to compute constrained paths PCEP / BGP SR Policy Southbound protocol to push computed SR Policies to headend routers

Common Pitfalls

  • BGP-LS SRv6 extensions only export information -- they do not program the data plane. A separate mechanism (PCEP, BGP SR Policy) is needed to install computed paths.
  • The BGP-LS speaker must participate in the IGP to learn SRv6 TLVs. If the IGP peering is misconfigured or the IGP does not carry SRv6 extensions, BGP-LS will have an incomplete view.
  • Ensure the BGP-LS speaker's IGP implementation supports the SRv6-specific TLVs from RFC 9352 (IS-IS) or RFC 9513 (OSPFv3). Older implementations may silently drop unknown TLVs.
  • The SRv6 SID Structure TLV is essential for the controller to correctly parse the LOC:FUNC:ARG boundaries. Missing SID Structure information can lead to incorrect path computation.
  • Multi-domain deployments require BGP-LS from each domain. The controller must stitch cross-domain topology views to compute end-to-end SRv6 paths.

docs.title: RFC 9800 - Compressed SRv6 Segment List Encoding
docs.text: Field Value Title Compressed SRv6 Segment List Encoding Authors C. Filsfils, Z. Ali, C. Previdi, F. Clad, et al. Date June 2025 Status Proposed Standard Link datatracker.ietf.org/doc/rfc9800

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
RFC 9800 defines the Compressed SRv6 Segment List Encoding, commonly known as micro-SID (uSID). This mechanism addresses one of the primary concerns with SRv6: the overhead of 128-bit SIDs in the Segment Routing Header. By packing multiple compressed SIDs (C-SIDs) into a single 128-bit container, the encoding dramatically reduces SRH overhead, making SRv6 competitive with SR-MPLS in terms of per-packet encapsulation cost.

The specification defines two SRv6 SID flavors: NEXT-C-SID and REPLACE-C-SID. Both flavors use a container structure where multiple short SID values (typically 16 or 32 bits) are packed into standard 128-bit SRv6 SID slots. The key operation is "shift-and-lookup": when a node processes its C-SID, it shifts the remaining C-SIDs within the container and performs a lookup on the next one, avoiding the need to decrement Segments Left for every micro-segment.

The compression is transparent to transit nodes, which see only a standard IPv6 destination address. This means uSID can be deployed incrementally -- only SR-capable endpoints need to support the compressed encoding, while the rest of the network forwards packets using standard IPv6.

docs.title: Key Sections
docs.text: Section Topic Description 3 C-SID Container Format How compressed SIDs are packed into 128-bit slots 4 NEXT-C-SID Flavor Processing using the Next-Header chain for container advancement 5 REPLACE-C-SID Flavor Processing by replacing within the active container 6 C-SID Shift-and-Lookup Core forwarding operation for both flavors 7 Interworking Mixing compressed and uncompressed SIDs in the same SRH

docs.title: C-SID Container Format
docs.text:
A single 128-bit SRv6 SID slot is divided into a Global Identifier Block (GIB) and a sequence of C-SIDs:

|<---------- 128 bits ---------->|
| GIB (variable) | C-SID1 | C-SID2 | C-SID3 | ... | padding |
Component Typical Size Purpose GIB 32 bits Common block prefix shared by all uSIDs in the domain C-SID 16 bits (default) Compressed node/function identifier Container 128 bits One Segment List entry holding multiple C-SIDs

With a 32-bit GIB and 16-bit C-SIDs, a single 128-bit container holds up to 6 micro-segments.

docs.title: NEXT-C-SID vs REPLACE-C-SID
docs.text: Property NEXT-C-SID REPLACE-C-SID Container advancement Decrements Segments Left to move to the next 128-bit container Replaces C-SIDs within the current container entry SL decrement On container boundary only On container boundary only Shift operation Shift C-SIDs left within the active container Same shift operation Empty container detection Next C-SID position is all zeros Same Interworking Can coexist with uncompressed SIDs Can coexist with uncompressed SIDs

docs.title: Overhead Comparison
docs.text: Encoding 4-Segment Path 8-Segment Path 12-Segment Path Uncompressed SRv6 64 bytes (4 x 16B) 128 bytes (8 x 16B) 192 bytes (12 x 16B) uSID (16-bit C-SIDs) 16 bytes (1 container) 32 bytes (2 containers) 32 bytes (2 containers) SR-MPLS 16 bytes (4 x 4B) 32 bytes (8 x 4B) 48 bytes (12 x 4B)

docs.title: Shift-and-Lookup Operation
docs.text:
When a uSID-capable node receives a packet:

  1. Extract its own C-SID from the current position in the active container
  2. Shift remaining C-SIDs left by one C-SID length within the container
  3. If the next C-SID position is all zeros (end of container), decrement Segments Left and move to the next container entry
  4. Write the updated container value into the IPv6 Destination Address
  5. Perform an IPv6 FIB lookup on the new Destination Address

Common Pitfalls

  • All nodes in a uSID domain must share the same GIB prefix and C-SID length. Mismatched configurations cause forwarding failures.
  • The "End of Container" condition (all-zeros in the next C-SID position) must be handled correctly. A C-SID value of 0 is reserved and must not be assigned to any node.
  • Mixing NEXT-C-SID and REPLACE-C-SID flavors within the same segment list is not supported. Choose one flavor per deployment.
  • Compressed and uncompressed SIDs can coexist in the same SRH, but the transition boundaries must be configured correctly.
  • Operators accustomed to SRv6 packet captures will see fewer Segment List entries than expected because multiple hops are packed into each container.

docs.title: RFC 9855 - TI-LFA
docs.text: Field Value Title Topology-Independent Loop-Free Alternate Fast Reroute Authors S. Litkowski, A. Bashandy, C. Filsfils, B. Decraene, P. Francois, D. Voyer Date June 2025 Status Proposed Standard Link datatracker.ietf.org/doc/rfc9855

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
RFC 9855 defines Topology-Independent Loop-Free Alternate (TI-LFA), a fast-reroute mechanism for IP and SR networks that provides sub-50ms protection against link, node, and SRLG (Shared Risk Link Group) failures. Unlike traditional LFA (RFC 5286) and Remote LFA (RFC 7490), TI-LFA guarantees 100% coverage for any topology -- hence "topology-independent."

TI-LFA computes backup paths that follow the post-convergence shortest path. When a failure occurs, the protecting node (Point of Local Repair, or PLR) immediately redirects traffic along a pre-computed repair path using a repair segment list. Because the repair path mirrors the post-convergence path, once the IGP reconverges, the traffic is already on the correct path, avoiding sub-optimal routing during the convergence window.

The mechanism relies on Segment Routing to encode the repair path as a short segment list pushed onto the packet at the PLR. This is done using prefix SIDs, adjacency SIDs, or a combination thereof. The key algorithmic concept is the P-node and Q-node (or PQ-node), which represents the point on the post-convergence path where the repair tunnel can safely deliver traffic.

docs.title: Key Sections
docs.text: Section Topic Description 3 TI-LFA Repair Tunnel How the post-convergence repair path is computed 4 P-Space and Q-Space Defining the reachability spaces from PLR and destination 5 PQ-Node Selection Finding the intersection where P-Space meets Q-Space 6 Segment List for Repair Encoding the repair path using SR segments 7 Link Protection Protecting against single link failures 8 Node Protection Protecting against node failures 9 SRLG Protection Protecting against shared risk link group failures

docs.title: P-Space, Q-Space, and PQ-Node
docs.text: Concept Definition P-Space Set of nodes reachable from the PLR without traversing the failed element Q-Space Set of nodes that can reach the destination without traversing the failed element (computed on the post-convergence topology) PQ-Node A node in the intersection of P-Space and Q-Space -- the ideal repair tunnel endpoint P-Node A node in P-Space but not Q-Space; requires an additional segment to reach Q-Space

docs.title: Repair Segment List Construction
docs.text: Scenario Repair Segments Needed Example Direct PQ-node exists 1 segment (Prefix SID to PQ-node) PLR pushes No direct PQ-node 2+ segments (P-node Prefix SID + Adjacency SID to Q-node) PLR pushes Adjacent PQ-node 1 segment (Adjacency SID) PLR pushes

docs.title: Protection Types
docs.text: Protection Level What is Protected Computation Link Protection Single link failure Post-convergence SPF excluding the protected link Node Protection Failure of a directly connected neighbor node Post-convergence SPF excluding the protected node and all its links SRLG Protection All links in a Shared Risk Link Group Post-convergence SPF excluding all links in the SRLG

docs.title: TI-LFA vs Other FRR Mechanisms
docs.text:
Mechanism Coverage Requires SR Post-Convergence Optimal Topology Dependent LFA (RFC 5286) Partial (~40-80%) No No Yes Remote LFA (RFC 7490) Better (~90-95%) No (uses LDP tunnel) No Yes TI-LFA 100% Yes Yes No

Common Pitfalls

  • TI-LFA requires Segment Routing to be enabled. Without SR, the repair segment list cannot be constructed.
  • Node protection is strictly stronger than link protection but may produce longer repair segment lists. Some platforms limit the number of repair segments they can push.
  • SRLG protection requires that SRLG membership be consistently configured across all nodes. Inconsistent SRLG definitions lead to inadequate protection.
  • The post-convergence path computation assumes all nodes reconverge to the same topology. Asymmetric link costs or misconfigured metrics can invalidate this assumption.
  • TI-LFA protects the data plane within 50ms, but the control plane still needs full IGP convergence. During the window between FRR activation and convergence, traffic follows the repair path, which may differ from the final converged path.

docs.title: 5G Transport with SRv6
docs.text: SRv6 (particularly uSID) is rapidly becoming the preferred transport technology for converged 5G xHaul networks — replacing legacy MPLS in backhaul, midhaul, and fronthaul segments.

docs.title: Why SRv6 for 5G?
docs.text: 5G Requirement SRv6 Solution Network Slicing SRv6 Flex-Algorithm enables per-slice path computation Low Latency SR Policies steer traffic on latency-optimized paths Scalability 128-bit SID space supports millions of endpoints Simplification No MPLS signaling (LDP, RSVP-TE) needed Convergence Single protocol for backhaul, midhaul, and fronthaul Automation Programmable SIDs enable intent-based networking

    DU -->|Midhaul| CU[Centralized Unit]

docs.title: SRv6 MUP (Mobile User Plane)
docs.text: One of the most significant innovations is SRv6 MUP (RFC 9433), pioneered by SoftBank. MUP replaces GTP-U tunnels with SRv6 segments, encoding mobile session information directly into SIDs.

docs.title: Before MUP (Traditional 5G)
docs.text: [IPv6][GTP-U][Inner IP][Payload]  ← Tunnel overhead

docs.title: With SRv6 MUP
docs.text:
[IPv6][SRH with MUP SIDs][Payload]  ← Native SRv6, no GTP-U

Benefits:

  • Eliminates GTP-U tunnel overhead
  • Enables end-to-end network slicing from RAN to core
  • Reduces cost and operational complexity
  • Enables seamless MEC (Multi-access Edge Computing) integration

docs.title: Public Deployment Announcements
docs.text:
Several operators have publicly announced SRv6 deployments for 5G transport:

  • SoftBank (Japan) — announced SRv6 Flex-Algo and MUP on their commercial 5G network (source)
  • Rakuten Mobile (Japan) — announced SRv6 uSID IP transport migration (source)
  • Iliad (Italy) — announced greenfield SRv6 5G-ready network with no MPLS (source)

See Real-World Deployments for a full directory with links to original announcements.

SRv6 uSID meets 5G transport requirements

SRv6 uSID has been validated against eCPRI, O-RAN Alliance, ITU-T, and 3GPP standards for latency, jitter, synchronization, and convergence per service slice.

docs.title: AI/ML Training Networks with SRv6
docs.text: SRv6 is emerging as a critical technology for AI backend fabrics — the networks connecting thousands of GPUs in training clusters. Traditional ECMP routing fails catastrophically for AI workloads, and SRv6 provides the solution.

docs.title: The Problem: ECMP Hash Collisions
docs.text:
AI training workloads (like LLM training) use synchronized all-to-all communication patterns across thousands of GPUs. This creates massive elephant flows that cause:

  • Hash collisions in traditional ECMP — multiple flows land on the same path
  • Congestion on some links while parallel paths sit completely idle
  • Training slowdowns — the entire job is only as fast as the slowest GPU
  • Wasted bandwidth — up to 40-60% of fabric capacity underutilized
graph TB
    subgraph Traditional ECMP - The Problem
        G1[GPU 1] -->|Flow A| S1[Spine 1]
        G2[GPU 2] -->|Flow B| S1
        G3[GPU 3] -->|Flow C| S1
        G4[GPU 4] -.->|Idle| S2[Spine 2]
    end

    style S1 fill:#ef5350,color:#fff
    style S2 fill:#4a148c,color:#fff,stroke:#ab47bc
    style G1 fill:#7b1fa2,color:#fff,stroke:#ab47bc
    style G2 fill:#7b1fa2,color:#fff,stroke:#ab47bc
    style G3 fill:#7b1fa2,color:#fff,stroke:#ab47bc
    style G4 fill:#7b1fa2,color:#fff,stroke:#ab47bc

docs.title: The Solution: SRv6 Deterministic Path Placement
docs.text:
With SRv6 uSID, the source (GPU host or controller) explicitly programs the exact path each flow takes through the fabric. No hashing, no collisions, no wasted capacity.

graph TB
    subgraph SRv6 Deterministic - The Solution
        G1[GPU 1] -->|SID→Spine1| S1[Spine 1]
        G2[GPU 2] -->|SID→Spine2| S2[Spine 2]
        G3[GPU 3] -->|SID→Spine3| S3[Spine 3]
        G4[GPU 4] -->|SID→Spine4| S4[Spine 4]
    end

    style S1 fill:#4a148c,color:#fff,stroke:#ab47bc
    style S2 fill:#4a148c,color:#fff,stroke:#ab47bc
    style S3 fill:#4a148c,color:#fff,stroke:#ab47bc
    style S4 fill:#4a148c,color:#fff,stroke:#ab47bc
    style G1 fill:#7b1fa2,color:#fff,stroke:#ab47bc
    style G2 fill:#7b1fa2,color:#fff,stroke:#ab47bc
    style G3 fill:#7b1fa2,color:#fff,stroke:#ab47bc
    style G4 fill:#7b1fa2,color:#fff,stroke:#ab47bc

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
1. Each spine/path is assigned an SRv6 uSID
  2. The GPU host (or a controller) encapsulates traffic with an SRH containing the specific spine SID
  3. Traffic is deterministically placed on the chosen path — zero hash collisions
  4. Load balancing is done by the application or controller, not by the network

docs.title: Key Benefits for AI
docs.text: Benefit Description Zero hash collisions Every flow is explicitly placed on a specific path 100% fabric utilization No idle paths while others are congested Application-aware GPUs can control their own path selection No protocol overhead uSID fits in a single 128-bit IPv6 address Works with existing hardware Runs on standard Broadcom/Cisco Silicon One ASICs

docs.title: Satellite Connectivity with SRv6
docs.text: As LEO (Low Earth Orbit) satellite constellations become viable for enterprise and telecom transport, SRv6 is emerging as the natural overlay technology for delivering carrier-grade services over satellite links.

docs.title: The Opportunity
docs.text: LEO satellites offer low-latency broadband to locations where fiber is impractical — remote sites, maritime, disaster recovery, temporary deployments. The challenge is delivering carrier-grade L2/L3 services reliably over this transport.

docs.title: Why SRv6 for Satellite?
docs.text: Challenge SRv6 Solution IPv6-native transport LEO constellations like Starlink use IPv6 natively — SRv6 fits without protocol translation Variable latency and jitter SR Policies can steer traffic across satellite + terrestrial paths based on real-time conditions Service delivery EVPN and L3VPN over SRv6 enable MEF-compliant Ethernet services end-to-end Resiliency Multiple segment lists provide fast failover between satellite and terrestrial backup paths Simplified operations No MPLS signaling needed over the satellite link Edge compute SRv6 service chaining enables MEC (Multi-access Edge Computing) at remote sites

docs.title: Architecture Concept
docs.text:
A typical SRv6-over-satellite architecture uses SRv6/EVPN as the overlay, with the satellite link as one of potentially multiple underlay transport options:

graph LR
    RS1[Remote Site 1] -->|LEO Satellite| HQ[Head Office / Hub]
    RS2[Remote Site 2] -->|LEO Satellite| HQ
    RS1 -.->|Terrestrial Backup| HQ

    subgraph SRv6/EVPN Overlay
        RS1
        RS2
        HQ
    end

    style RS1 fill:#7b1fa2,color:#fff,stroke:#ab47bc
    style RS2 fill:#7b1fa2,color:#fff,stroke:#ab47bc
    style HQ fill:#4a148c,color:#fff,stroke:#ab47bc

docs.title: Key Design Elements
docs.text:
• Dual transport: Satellite as primary, terrestrial (4G/LTE, MPLS, internet) as backup
  • MEF L2 EVCs: Ethernet Virtual Connections with primary and backup paths for resiliency
  • SRv6/EVPN overlay: Unified service delivery regardless of underlay transport type
  • Performance monitoring: Assurance sensors at each site for SLA validation
  • Compact CPE: Low-power routers at remote sites suited for space-constrained deployments

docs.title: Applicable SRv6 Behaviors
docs.text: Behavior Use in Satellite Scenario End.DT4 / End.DT6 VRF termination at hub site End.DX2 Point-to-point L2 service (E-Line) across satellite End.DT2 Multipoint L2 service (E-LAN) for branch connectivity H.Encaps.Red Reduced encapsulation to minimize overhead on bandwidth-constrained satellite links

docs.title: Industry Validation
docs.text:
Multiple vendors and operators have demonstrated or deployed SRv6 over satellite transport, including validated architectures combining compact router platforms with LEO satellite connectivity for enterprise and telecom use cases. These are typically showcased at industry events such as Cisco Live and Mobile World Congress.

Overhead matters over satellite

Satellite bandwidth is expensive. SRv6 uSID compression (RFC 9800) is especially valuable here — it reduces SRv6 overhead by up to 83% compared to classic SRv6, making it practical even on bandwidth-constrained satellite links.

docs.title: Service Chaining with SRv6
docs.text: Service Function Chaining (SFC) is the ability to steer traffic through an ordered set of network functions — firewalls, IDS/IPS, load balancers, DPI engines, NAT, WAN optimizers, etc. SRv6 provides one of the most elegant solutions for SFC by encoding the entire service chain as a segment list.

docs.title: The Problem with Traditional SFC
docs.text:
Without SRv6, service chaining typically relies on:

  • Manual VLAN/VRF stitching — each service function connected via dedicated VLANs, brittle and hard to change
  • Policy-Based Routing (PBR) — complex ACLs at every hop, doesn't scale
  • NSH (Network Service Header) — RFC 8300 overlay, requires all functions to support NSH
  • Traffic steering appliances — dedicated boxes that redirect traffic, adding cost and latency

These approaches share common pain points:

Problem Impact Topology dependent Service functions must be physically or logically adjacent Static chains Changing the order requires reconfiguring multiple devices State overhead Per-flow state at every classifier and service function Scalability Each new service in the chain multiplies complexity

docs.title: How SRv6 Solves Service Chaining
docs.text: With SRv6, the entire service chain is expressed as a segment list in the packet header. The ingress node encapsulates the packet once, and the network steers it through each function automatically.

docs.title: Key Principle
docs.text:
Each service function (or the node hosting it) is assigned an SRv6 SID. The segment list defines the chain:

Segment List: [FW::1, IDS::1, LB::1, Egress::DT4]
                 ↓        ↓        ↓         ↓
              Firewall → IDS → Load Balancer → Decap & deliver

The packet traverses the chain by following the SRv6 forwarding mechanics (decrement Segments Left, update DA, forward).

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
graph LR
    C[Client] --> I[Ingress PE]
    I -->|"DA=FW::1<br/>SL=3"| FW[Firewall]
    FW -->|"DA=IDS::1<br/>SL=2"| IDS[IDS/IPS]
    IDS -->|"DA=LB::1<br/>SL=1"| LB[Load Balancer]
    LB -->|"DA=Egress::DT4<br/>SL=0"| E[Egress PE]
    E --> S[Server]

    style C fill:#1a1a3e,color:#e1bee7,stroke:#ab47bc
    style S fill:#1a1a3e,color:#e1bee7,stroke:#ab47bc
    style I fill:#7b1fa2,color:#fff,stroke:#ab47bc
    style E fill:#7b1fa2,color:#fff,stroke:#ab47bc
    style FW fill:#4a148c,color:#fff,stroke:#ab47bc
    style IDS fill:#4a148c,color:#fff,stroke:#ab47bc
    style LB fill:#4a148c,color:#fff,stroke:#ab47bc

docs.title: Packet Walk: Service Chain
docs.text: Let's trace a packet through a chain: Client → Firewall → IDS → Server

docs.title: Step 1: Ingress Classification
docs.text:
The ingress PE matches the traffic (e.g., by source IP, DSCP, VRF) and encapsulates with an SRv6 header containing the service chain:

Original:
  [IPv4: Client → Server][Payload]

After encapsulation:
  [IPv6: Ingress → FW::1]
  [SRH: SL=2, List=[Egress::DT4, IDS::1, FW::1]]
  [IPv4: Client → Server]
  [Payload]

docs.title: Step 2: Firewall (SRv6 Proxy)
docs.text:
The packet arrives at the firewall node. The SRv6 behavior depends on whether the firewall is SRv6-aware or not:

SRv6-Aware FunctionSRv6-Unaware Function (Proxy)

The firewall natively understands SRv6. It processes the packet, inspects the inner payload, applies its policy, and forwards with Segments Left decremented:

FW receives:  DA=FW::1, SL=2
FW processes:  Inspect payload, apply firewall policy
FW forwards:   DA=IDS::1, SL=1

Most existing network functions don't understand SRv6. SRv6 proxy behaviors solve this by stripping the SRv6 header before the function and re-adding it after:

Proxy receives:  [IPv6+SRH][IPv4][Payload]
Proxy strips:    [IPv4][Payload]  ← Sent to firewall
Firewall processes: Normal IPv4 inspection
Proxy re-adds:   [IPv6+SRH][IPv4][Payload]  ← SRH restored
Proxy forwards:  DA=IDS::1, SL=1

docs.title: Step 3: IDS
docs.text: Same process — the IDS inspects the packet (directly or via proxy), then the packet continues to the next SID.

docs.title: Step 4: Egress Decapsulation
docs.text: The egress PE matches Egress::DT4 (End.DT4 behavior), removes the SRv6 encapsulation, and delivers the original IPv4 packet to the server.

docs.title: SRv6 Proxy Behaviors
docs.text:
The key innovation for service chaining is the set of proxy behaviors defined in the IETF SR Service Programming draft. These allow SRv6-unaware functions to participate in an SRv6 service chain:

Behavior Name How It Works End.AS Static Proxy Caches the SRH, strips it before the function, restores it after. Static: one cached SRH per SID. End.AD Dynamic Proxy Like End.AS but dynamically caches the SRH per-packet. Supports functions that modify the inner packet. End.AM Masquerading Proxy Modifies the outer IPv6 DA to the function's address while caching the SRH. The function sees a normal IPv6 packet and routes it back.

docs.title: End.AD (Dynamic Proxy) — Most Common
docs.text:
sequenceDiagram
    participant Net as Network
    participant Proxy as SRv6 Proxy Node
    participant SF as Service Function

    Net->>Proxy: IPv6+SRH+Inner packet
    Proxy->>Proxy: Cache SRH, strip outer header
    Proxy->>SF: Inner packet only (IPv4/IPv6)
    SF->>SF: Process (firewall, IDS, etc.)
    SF->>Proxy: Processed inner packet
    Proxy->>Proxy: Restore SRH, update SL
    Proxy->>Net: IPv6+SRH+Inner packet (next SID)

This is critical because it means any existing network function (physical or virtual) can be inserted into an SRv6 service chain without modification.

docs.title: Dynamic Chain Modification
docs.text:
One of SRv6's most powerful advantages for SFC: changing the chain requires only updating the segment list at the ingress. No reconfiguration of intermediate nodes or service functions.

Operation What Changes Add a service Insert a new SID in the segment list Remove a service Remove the SID from the segment list Reorder services Change the order of SIDs Bypass a service Skip its SID (e.g., during maintenance) A/B test Different segment lists for different traffic classes

All of this happens at the ingress only — the rest of the network and the service functions themselves are unchanged.

docs.title: Advantages over Traditional SFC
docs.text: Aspect Traditional SFC SRv6 Service Chaining Topology dependency Functions must be adjacent Functions can be anywhere Chain modification Reconfigure every hop Update segment list at ingress only Per-flow state in network Required at every classifier None (stateless forwarding) Legacy function support Often requires function modification Proxy behaviors (End.AS/AD/AM) Metadata transport NSH or custom headers SRH TLVs TE integration Separate mechanism Native (SR Policies) Scalability O(functions × classifiers) O(1) — just a segment list

docs.title: SFC + Traffic Engineering
docs.text:
SRv6 uniquely combines service chaining with traffic engineering in a single segment list:

Segment List: [LowLatencySpine::1, FW::1, IDS::1, Egress::DT4]
               └── TE segment ──┘  └── Service chain ──────────┘

The same packet can be steered through a specific network path and a specific service chain, all encoded in one SRH.

  4. RFC 8300 - Network Service Header (NSH) - Alternative SFC approach for comparison with SRv6-based service chaining

docs.title: Traffic Engineering with SRv6
docs.text: SRv6 Traffic Engineering (TE) enables operators to steer traffic along explicit paths through the network, optimizing for latency, bandwidth, or other constraints.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
Instead of relying on shortest-path routing alone, SRv6 TE defines SR Policies that specify explicit segment lists (paths) for traffic.

graph LR
    A[Ingress PE] -->|Low Latency Path| B[P1]
    B --> C[P2]
    C --> D[Egress PE]
    A -->|High BW Path| E[P3]
    E --> F[P4]
    F --> D
    style A fill:#7b1fa2,color:#fff,stroke:#ab47bc
    style D fill:#7b1fa2,color:#fff,stroke:#ab47bc
    style B fill:#4a148c,color:#fff,stroke:#ab47bc
    style C fill:#4a148c,color:#fff,stroke:#ab47bc
    style E fill:#4a148c,color:#fff,stroke:#ab47bc
    style F fill:#4a148c,color:#fff,stroke:#ab47bc

docs.title: SR Policy Components
docs.text: Component Description Headend The node where the policy is applied Color Identifies the intent (e.g., low-latency) Endpoint Destination of the policy Candidate Path One or more segment lists for the policy Segment List Ordered list of SIDs defining the path

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• Low-latency routing for real-time applications
  • Bandwidth reservation for critical flows
  • Disjoint paths for redundancy
  • WAN optimization across multiple domains

docs.title: Further Reading
docs.text:
• VPN Services

docs.text:

  3. Configure SR-TE Policies - Cisco 8000 Series, IOS XR - Cisco IOS XR configuration guide for SR-TE policies on Cisco 8000 series routers

docs.title: VPN Services with SRv6
docs.text: SRv6 provides a native mechanism to deliver L3VPN and L2VPN services without MPLS, using SRv6 SIDs as service identifiers.

docs.title: SRv6-based L3VPN
docs.text: SRv6 L3VPN uses End.DT4 / End.DT6 behaviors to decapsulate traffic and perform a lookup in the appropriate VRF table.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
1. PE router receives customer IPv4/IPv6 traffic
  2. Encapsulates with outer IPv6 header + SRH
  3. Sets destination to the remote PE's VPN SID (End.DT4)
  4. Remote PE decapsulates and forwards to the correct VRF

BGP VPNv4 with SRv6

BGP Update:
  Prefix: 10.0.0.0/24
  Next-Hop: 2001:db8::2
  SRv6 SID: fc00:0:2::DT4  (End.DT4 behavior)
  VRF: CUSTOMER-A

docs.text: Aspect MPLS L3VPN SRv6 L3VPN Label signaling MP-BGP + LDP/RSVP MP-BGP only Service identifier VPN label SRv6 SID (End.DT4/DT6) Transport MPLS LSP IPv6 native Complexity Higher (multiple protocols) Lower (IPv6 + BGP)
